sentences.text: The last word is [NON-ENGLISH  SPEECH].
That means outside it's cold and windy.
And that is the mother's voice talking to herself.
And she is saying she is-- she feels like Moses' mother felt, that she had no choice.
And she left her child on strange hands-- strange arms to live with.
Yes.
I heard that song that young man-- he was a young man-- sing it as a concert.
And somehow, it stuck in my memory.
I don't know why.
I mean, he sang a few songs.
And I cannot-- don't remember the words.
Because when you say that song, the other song-- there was a song also, which my granddaughter was in a play.
And she had to do just what the other song said.
She says, Grandma, I am in a play.
And I'm doing something which is not important enough.
I says, what do you mean?
She told me in the song, her mother is standing there.
Pack.
Pack the clothes.
You're in charge of taking the clothes.
While we were taking away, we knew we were [INAUDIBLE].
No, I just want to-- I just want to tell you the few words about the song.
From what I understood after the war, there were [? chayalim. ?] And there was in Poland all over underground.
They were trying to take out the youth as many as they could.
There were a lot of people involved helping them out to get the young people.
There was, we foun-- I found out after the war quite a few non-Jewish families were hiding those people, people or kids.
So mostly the [? chayalim, ?] but also working with the underground.
It wasn't up to me.
I don't know.
I've been asking the same question.
How come-- I didn't belong to anyone.
How come they didn't include me?
You see, where I came from, they were-- they kept things away from the children.
They did not want not to expose them that it's something bad.
Everything is perfect.
Everything will work out.
So I asked my question, the same question, a million times.
But I have no answers.
It was something, 1944-- must have been like April, I think March, April, that they finally took us to the ghetto, the [?
Kosice ?] ghetto.
It was in spring.
It was in the springtime.
Because I know we celebrated Passover in the ghetto.
In ghetto, we were working.
I was working in a brick factory with carrying bricks, fixing up bricks.
And we were-- they put us all up in a factory.
And of course, we're already on the the floor.
They did give us-- we had blankets at that time with us.
So we were there.
I don't remember-- we were not staying there for very-- for very long.
In the brick factory.
From there they--
The brick fact-- we were in the ghetto and working in a brick factory.
Now, the ghetto was in the brick factory.
So we were working--
Yes.
Yes.
We were stay-- yes.
All of us.
Some of them had cots.
But most of us were sleeping on the floor.
I don't know.
I wouldn't know.
I was a stranger over there.
So I really don't know how big the ghetto was.
But I remember there was a very big, like long barracks.
And they were-- we put on sheets to separate families from families.
But your family had a little two sheets on both sides.
And there you were, like staying on-- laying on the floor.
Any other facilities, I don't remember.
And what the food was, I have no idea.
I just don't-- can't recall what it's-- what's what.
Somehow, the food wasn't important.
It was just part of living.
And we were just hoping tomorrow will be better, something new.
We walked, within walking distance from where the camp was.
It was in walking distance.
By trucks-- I, I think it must have been the Germans which they took us from the-- from the poor-- from the children's home to the ghetto on trucks.
They loaded us up on trucks, in open trucks.
And I don't remember how far it was.
Well, at that time, we-- the main thing that we took was backpacks.
Used to call them [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
They just came up here in, in America.
We packed then.
And I think that one suitcase, they allowed you to take.
From--
From the home-- I wasn't in the home.
I was living in, in the [? poor. ?] I was living in the children's home.
I was living there.
She used to travel home, back and forth.
But I was living there.
It was really nice.
They-- they gave me a little room.
And it was very nice, because she lived in a small apartment.
And just-- there wasn't enough room for me.
So I, I stayed in there.
So she was with me all day-- all day long.
And in the morning, she came back.
So it gave me the evenings that I'd let us study.
I-- when you are young, you don't think of it.
You just keep yourself busy.
Maybe that's why I have the claustrophobia now, staying in that little tiny, room.
Never-- never thought about it this way.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Yes.
Was just a few months.
After that, they loaded us on trains.
The trains.
And we went--
The Germans.
They put us in trains.
And we were traveling.
It was quite, quite a days or week, or a week or so, we were in the train.
They were cattle trains.
And the facilities in the cattle trains which we had was a blanket holding by two ladies.
At that time, we were separated already-- ladies and men separate-- with a bucket, whatever you had to go for your-- Food I don't remember.
The food, whatever we had, we took it along with us.
But I don't remember them feeding us.
So every time at, at night when the train stopped, somebody had to empty bucket.
The facilities, the trains were so small that they made us-- we had, we had an SS man staying by the door with a rifle in case somebody decide to jump off.
And at night, we had to lay down.
It so tight that we had-- that we were like herring, her-- put together.
If one turned, everybody turned.
But-- and we had some straw.
They were very nice to us.
They gave us some straw on the train, so at least we were not flat.
And--
I was in the s-- I don't remember.
But I remember when we got off in Auschwitz.
I did not know where we were going.
We were just in the train.
We were young kids.
We had there a group kids.
We, we were working in the factory.
We were learning new songs and carrying on.
We made, we made a party.
And always Jews, when we get together, two, three of them, we make a party.
We sing.
And we dance.
And we carry on.
So--
In the brick factory.
We had there some actors that were teaching the songs.
So while we were working, we had to carry bricks from one room to the other.
We made a chain.
And we're just throwing the bricks to one another.
While we're throwing, we're singing.
So we made--
Made a joke out of this.
It went like this.
[SINGING IN HUNGARIAN] That's Hungarian.
I live in the brick factory.
And my hair is covered with dust.
But we were kids at that time.
With that-- we made the best of it.
Let's put it this way.
And we had there two actors.
And they were entertaining us while they were also working, they made-- make it look like fun.
In fact, when we went on the train, they made up a song right away.
They went like this.
[SINGING IN HUNGARIAN].
I'm gonna explain it to you.
This is Hungarian.
The train is moving.
The chimney-- the smoke is coming out of the chimney.
And it's taking away all the Jews.
And who knows if we will ever-- whoever is coming back.
So you know, like you made the best of the situation that we have.
And I always did.
I guess that's why I am where I am.
If not, I would have never survived.
When we get out of the train in Auschwitz--
It must have been like at least more than a week.
I really don't remember, because we traveled at night.
And sometimes the trains in the daytime, they were just staying in the stations.
They did not want, when there was a air raid, that there is a train going to Auschwitz, so depended when.
Sometimes they were traveling early in the morning and late at night and all through the night.
In the daytime, sometimes they did travel during the day.
But sometimes they were just staying in the station.
The spring.
It was just after Passover.
Must have something around May or something.
The Auschwitz, we got off the train.
And there were the SS men.
And [INAUDIBLE] I wish I would made it my business to remember the names.
And they were just staying the way they are dressed, and with a stick, you, here.
You, here.
You, here.
They were just picking you one, two, three.
And of course, if you had somebody, you wanted to be s-- with somebody.
If they wanted to go like with a sister, with a mother, or with dad, and they-- right away, you could feel the-- oh, they hit them with a club.
Or they hit them with a gun.
Or they hit them there that they were able to remain on the floor, which they didn't go to any line at all.
They were [? buried ?] off.
Well, when I got to Auschwitz, I was staying with my mother's friend, Mrs.
Vokosh, ?] her daughter, and myself.
I wasn't very long in Auschwitz.
They were wearing the tall boots.
Sp-- pants were like riding, like riding pants.
And clothes were, from what I remember, I think green and gray, and the hats, those SS hats.
And of course, the big SS were right on their, on their sleeves.
And they have the [INAUDIBLE] medallions hanging on, on their jackets.
That's all I can describe.
They took us to a bathhouse.
Going from the train to the bathhouse, that was the worst experience which anybody can, can describe.
We were all walking in a line.
And there were people behind the bars already.
And we were thinking, who are those people?
We thought they was some kind of a criminals.
Because like I said, Mrs.
Vokosh ?] kept us in the dark.
She never wanted to talk about it-- what's what, what's ahead of us-- one day at a time, wait.
Well, she was also a very religious person.
One day at a time-- God will help you.
Don't worry.
Everything will be OK.
Well, they took us to the bathhouse.
They shaved our heads, our privates.
They put us in the showers with some kind of a disinfectant.
And just that shaving wasn't too pleasant, because the men did the job.
And here I was staying and looking at my friend.
I didn't recognize her without the hair.
Then they gave us dresses, which were gray and blue dresses, after the shower.
Stripes.
They put us in stripes, gray and blue stripes.
Gray and blue-- of course, they gave us whatever came first.
Somebody got dresses they were above the knees.
Somebody got dresses over the ankle.
But we kept-- after that, we switched them around, what's what.
They didn't care what was happening.
By the way, they took out all our belongings remained on the train.
They never allowed us to take them.
They did not allow us to take our belongings.
And some people who tried to sneak away something with us, once they took away everything from us, they had to leave them where the-- when they cut our hair.
And they took our clothes-- clothes, jewelry, whatever you had left.
Then we went in to one of the barracks that was in Auschwitz.
I don't remember which camp.
We were there for two nights.
Then they moved us-- moved us to another one closer to the crematorium, they told us.
And then we went to a third one, which we were sure that was our last night there.
They put us in a room, which the floor was wet.
They had washed it before, in cement.
And you better sit down or lay down.
If not, you are being shot.
That's all what we had.
There was a little window.
The little window was there.
They had a light constantly going on us, round and round, to check how everybody is.
And they were sitting and having a good time, the Germans.
And they were telling us, you better have a good time tonight.
That's the last night you're here.
They were drinking and eating, the SS men, in that little room-- at the guard house.
Whatev-- well, they made fun of us, all right?
They made fun of us, you know, called us all kind of a names.
This is the last night.
Tomorrow, you will see.
You will have a nice [INAUDIBLE].
So, now they had there--
That was in German.
In German-- we understood German.
We speak Yiddish.
We understood German.
But we thought it was our last night.
And there was a few religious women.
And they said prayers with us.
All night long, we're praying.
They all said the [?
Viduy-- ?] you know, if you know, before the dying.
I don't know-- and the religious people, you say [?
Viduy ?] before-- If you know you are going, you are ready to accept.
So we were not allowed to sing, but quietly, whispering, were praying all night long.
Nobody slept.
As it happened, the next morning they took us all out.
And they lined us up outside.
And they called.
And they told us, line up according to your alphabet, what your name is with.
At that time, we just didn't think straight.
It was just so numb.
So my friend tells me, look, your, your name starts with H, which is [?
Halpert. ?] Ours starts with F.
So you have to go to the, to the line which is with the H.
Well, that was the time which I was more alone than ever in the world.
But we were-- I was lucky because my line, the H, they took us on trains.
And they took us-- we went down to Riga.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Riga is in-- let me see-- Lithuania.
We came into Lithuania.
There was a big camp.
And there were people from the vicinities who they took them in.
It was also a ghetto.
And they still had their hair and their regular clothes.
They had their regular clothes.
When we came there, they all said, who are you?
Are you prisoners?
I mean, they were in the ghetto, but none of them-- not many people really knew what was going on.
Never saw them again.
No, I never saw them again.
But when were in-- when you are with people and you have nobody, you make friends.
You learn.
You make friends.
While we were there, we worked different, different places.
In Riga.
They took us to the boat.
That-- that's by the big port.
They took us to unload boats, unload trains.
And then another time, they-- we worked in a factory which they called [?
Anoden. ?]
Anoden. ?]
Anoden. ?] We took [INAUDIBLE].
All ?] [? right. ?] We took the old batteries.
And we cut them to took out the powder.
You take an old battery which we use for anything.
You cut out the back.
And then you split it.
And there is a little black powder.
So they needed the powder for the munition.
So there were three shifts working that every eight hours.
So we worked.
And, and our shift was-- our shift was, was at night.
They took us there by, by truck, open truck.
We came back-- whoever came back was black from the dust.
Well, we used to make fun of the people when we went in the morning to work and they were coming.
Where did you work?
They were black, which forget about showers.
If you were able to get washed up, you were very lucky.
So the people who worked at night were sleeping in the daytime.
They gave us-- they gave us lots of soup when we came in.
They gave you soup?
They gave us soup.
The portion usually was soup and like two slices of bread.
That was your da-- your daily allowance.
So if you ate up the soup and the bread for the day, you didn't eat nothing till the next 24 hours.
So if you were satisfied with the soup, save the bread-- if they didn't steal it from you overnight.
Because when you put it under your pillow, you didn't have anything that [? stopped ?] people.
I was lucky, because we had bunks.
I had bunks.
So at nights, while I was working, somebody else sleeped-- slept in it, which-- Oh.
So it was a bunk for yourself?
It was a bunk for myself.
They were like the three tiered bunks, one on top of the other.
And, and you had a bunk.
So when, when one the girls-- when I was working, she slept in it.
And then when she was working, I slept in it.
I mean, we didn't have our own.
To the same bunk, yes.
That was in Riga.
They were barracks.
They were barracks.
They're military barracks.
I was thinking-- in a-- we just one in a bed.
You only had one in a bed.
But being there were three shifts, so there was something or somebody came in early, somebody [? left. ?] So we did shared.
We had to share, two of us, the same bed.
And we were lucky because we had-- I don't remember what kind of mattress-- but we had a blanket and a pillow.
So we were very lucky.
And of course, if another girl came in, we had to share the same thing.
We still lived in the gray and blue stripes.
And if you're gonna ask me what kind of shoes, I don't remember what we had.
I think they might have let us keep the shoes.
And then they-- there were holes.
And then we had to put newspaper in it to keep it from going.
They never gave us new shoes.
But it wasn't bad there.
We were happy.
We had a, a place where we had to stay.
And then when the war started to get a little bit closer, the people who were there in the ghetto, they start taking them away.
Then they start separating, which they blamed us for coming there.
They started-- they separated them to send them to Auschwitz, the older people.
When we got in that camp, it was a camp, so-- but there were people who were there before.
They still had their own clothes.
So one morning, they called up all those people for an appell.
And they separated them.
And they send them away.
Very few of them remained.
Only the young people remained.
So--
OK.
Try to do my best.
It's not easy.
